data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1018 ET - Eli Lilly showed its weight-loss treatments are still in high demand, but less-than-stellar results from a study on an experimental anti-obesity pill is what investors are focusing on. The drug maker saw 2Q results jump from a year earlier, prompting it to raise its outlook for the year. Boosting the performance were robust sales for Mounjaro and Zepbound, rising 68% to $5.2 billion and more than doubling to $3.38 billion, respectively. But a study of Eli Lilly's orforglipron, the experimental anti-obesity pill, showed it helped people lose up to about 12% of their body weight after more than a year of treatment, which fell short of analysts' predictions of between 13% and 15% or more. Eli Lilly tumbles 14%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0824 ET - Germany's Merck KGaA reported a messy set of second-quarter results, UBS analysts say in a research note. Currency headwinds and one-time charges in its electronics segment offset an encouraging recovery in life science and a solid performance in healthcare, they note. "The key driver of the miss was one-time items in the electronics division which overshadowed continued solid recovery in life science and a strong profit performance at healthcare," the analysts say. The German life science-and-chemical group adjusted its full-year guidance to accommodate those divisional trends, but also raised its expectations of a currency hit and included recent acquisitions and business sales. "Overall, [the group's 2025] targets are broadly unchanged, with the better performing divisions absorbing the drags," they add. Shares fall 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0513 ET - European luxury companies delivered another sluggish quarter, which prompted worries among many investors around the long-term growth potential of the industry, UBS analysts write in a research note. The limited visibility on third-quarter trends, as well as currency headwinds, seem to have delayed the prospect of a returning earnings momentum after now almost two years of downgrades, the analysts say. UBS expects a sector recovery no sooner than in the second half of 2026. This is due to sector-specific challenges and macroeconomic and political issues, they say. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0404 ET - Germany's Merck KGaA reported second-quarter results that fell short of expectations at the top and bottom lines due to a weaker performance from the group's electronics segment, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. The German life-sciences and chemicals group reported net sales that were 1% below consensus estimates, and Ebitda before exceptional items that missed forecasts by 4%, the analysts say. Stronger expectations for Merck's life-science and healthcare segments lead the group to raise its full-year guidance for organic growth in adjusted Ebitda, Morgan Stanley says. The revised guidance implies a 2% upgrade to consensus estimates for Merck's full-year adjusted Ebitda, but a 1% cut to sales forecasts, according to Morgan Stanley. Shares fall 3.8%.(adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0304 ET - Sandoz's first-half sales came in slightly ahead of expectations, with growth driven by its biosimilars products and by a strong performance in Europe that offset U.S. weakness, Vontobel's Stefan Schneider says in a research note. "Biosimilars is where the growth is and from a regional perspective, the music plays in Europe--while the U.S. fell short of expectations," Schneider says. The Swiss generic-drug maker reported double-digit sales growth for its biosimilar products--copycat biologic drugs--in the first half and reiterated its full-year guidance. While Sandoz's products are exempted from tariffs for now, its guidance would hold up if it had to live with a 15% tariff on imports from the EU into the U.S., Vontobel says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0633 ET - The cost of insuring Swiss banks' credit against default using credit default swaps is unchanged as the 39% tariffs on Swiss imports into the U.S. come into effect. Investors expect the Swiss government to reach a favorable trade deal with the U.S. government. "The strength seen on Thursday for stocks in the region does serve to highlight an optimism that efforts to renegotiate the tariff rate will soon bear fruit," Rostro's Joshua Mahony says in a note. The 5-year credit default swap spreads on Swiss bank UBS Group remain unchanged at 48 basis points, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0537 ET - Raiffeisen Bank International has been delivering improving operating trends and profitability in the past 12 months, UBS says in a research note, raising its rating on the stock to buy from neutral. The Austrian lender's second-quarter results showed that net interest income held up better than expected, fee generation improved and that other income also seems higher, while loan loss provisions remained at muted levels, analysts Mate Nemes and Nicolas O'Sullivan write. Consensus expectations don't appropriately reflect the higher prospective return on tangible equity, they say. Additionally, Raiffeisen could benefit if its planned counter-claim against Russian investment firm Rasperia is successful, they add. UBS also lifts its target share price to 31 euros from 20.6 euros. Shares jump 11% to 27.56 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0508 ET - DBS's increasing dividend payout should set it apart from peers, Citi's Yong Hong Tan says in a note. The Singapore bank's quarterly payout rises to S$0.75 a share, compared with S$0.54 a year ago. DBS has committed to dividend growth and has a visible dividend pathway over 2025-2027, he says. DBS is also continuing its plan to buy back S$3 billion in shares. Tan expects the lender to buy these shares back at a slower pace if the share price stays elevated. Citi raises its target price on DBS to S$56.50 from S$48.10 and maintains its buy rating. Shares are 1.8% higher at S$49.75 after touching an intraday high of S$50. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0258 ET - Though Allianz faced headwinds from foreign-exchange fluctuations in the quarter, these didn't affect its print much, JPMorgan says in a research note. The German insurer reported operating profit for the second quarter ahead of expectations on growth in its property and casualty, as well as its life and health insurance divisions. Despite the material impacts of currency moves in the period, assets under management were in line with expectations. This suggests that consensus adjusted quickly to the foreign changes, which were a concern ahead of the results, analysts write. Contractual service margin--a component of an insurer's liabilities--also came in as expected despite being exposed to forex effects, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0246 ET - The weakness in Zurich Insurance's U.S. commercial lines is likely to be in focus, Citi says in a note after the Swiss group's first-half print. The combined operating ratio--a measure of profitability--for the division only advanced by a modest 20 basis points despite strong improvements in some of the turnaround areas, which was offset by deterioration elsewhere, analysts James A. Shuck and Prathamesh Chavan write. This will raise questions around the core operations of that business given that Zurich raised prices in North America. "Elsewhere things look fine and mostly in line, with Retail [gross written premium] growth a highlight and Farmers returning to policy growth with very strong surplus," they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0242 ET - There is nothing to complain about in Zurich Insurance's first-half results at first glance, Vontobel says in a research note. The Swiss insurer reported a good set of results that broadly aligned with consensus expectations, analyst Simon Foessmeier writes. Business operating profit rose 6% on year, or 7% in local currencies, illustrating the surprisingly small impact from foreign exchange fluctuations, he says. This is despite the fact that Zurich reports in U.S. dollars, a currency which has weakened in the period. "Given the high expectations, these results will unlikely move the shares much," he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1849 ET [Dow Jones]--Private fund manager TPG raised $11.3 billion during the 2Q, including $5.4 billion for credit strategies, nearly 80% more than in the same period of 2024 and the second- highest fundraising quarter in the Fort Worth-based firm's history. TPG expects to raise "significantly" more capital this year than in 2024, despite the fundraising hurdles the overall industry still faces, CEO Jon Winkelried says during an earnings call. Investors "remain cautious and highly selective amidst ongoing macro uncertainty and muted distributions," he says, referring to the difficulty that buyout firms in general are having to sell assets and return capital to investors. He notes, however, that TPG generated $6.5 billion in asset sales in 2Q, 20% more than a year earlier. (luis.garcia@wsj.com)

1828 ET - NZX's newest bear says the market is overestimating its sustainable growth level. Forsyth Barr downgrades NZX to underperform from neutral, noting the operator of New Zealand's stock exchange is trading at a higher multiple than its pre-Covid average. It says increased competition in the low-fee passive funds industry has heightened fee pressure and softened net inflows to NZX's high-value KiwiSaver product. It also reckons the headwinds to earnings growth in NZX's capital markets segment in the past decade will persist rather than ease. "Over the last five years, NZX's Ebitda mix has shifted from 90% markets to 40% funds management," analyst Benjamin Crozier says. "With funds management generating lower returns, more cyclical earnings, and higher competitive pressures, it represents a lower-multiple earnings stream." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Famine Is Unfolding in Gaza As Acute Malnutrition Surges: Over 20,000 Children Hospitalized
data.description:
Published by Action Against Hunger.

Please contact media@actionagainsthunger.org for inquiries.

NEW YORK, NY and JERUSALEM, ISRAEL / ACCESS Newswire / August 7, 2025 / The latest data from the Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) confirms the worst-case scenario in Gaza: famine indicator thresholds have been reached in most of the Gaza Strip. Since April, more than 20,000 children have been hospitalized for acute malnutrition. At least 16 children under the age of five have died from hunger-related causes since 17 July, and 3,000 more are in critical condition.

For months now, Action Against Hunger teams on the ground have been witnessing the calamity. "Famine is not just a statistic. It is the result of a slow and painful process that shrinks organs, collapses the immune system, and impairs cognitive abilities," explains Natalia Anguera, Action Against Hunger's Head of Operations for the Middle East. "Every day that passes without full and safe access to food, we are condemning thousands of people to avoidable suffering."

In July, more than 25% of pregnant and breastfeeding women assessed by Action Against Hunger teams were malnourished, representing a 16% increase from the previous month. According to OCHA, nearly 100% of children between 6 and 23 months and pregnant and breastfeeding women are unable to meet basic nutritional needs. As a result, 300,000 children under five and 150,000 women urgently need therapeutic nutritional support.

"The first thing I saw when I entered Gaza was destroyed buildings and areas with no signs of life," said one technical specialist in Gaza. "In the displacement camps, children collected food in silence, avoided eye contact and retreated to their tents, trying to protect what dignity they had left in a situation that had taken so much from them."

Humanitarian Access, Not Logistics, Is the Barrier

"No new aid delivery model will work - not a dock, not an air drop, not an isolated center - unless the siege is lifted completely and permanently," Anguera emphasized. "Humanitarian access is the problem, not logistics,"

Without full access, aid will remain insufficient. The little aid that does arrive is often unsuitable because it requires fuel and clean water for preparation, both of which are virtually non-existent in the Strip. Anguera adds that "Current distribution points are far away, dangerous to reach, and operate on a first-come-first-served basis, which excludes the most vulnerable."

Families in Gaza resorted to extreme coping strategies months ago, such as skipping meals, rationing bread, borrowing food, begging, and scavenging through rubbish. But these strategies are no longer done to stretch resources - they are done as desperate acts of survival.

Action Against Hunger's Response Amid Scarcity

Despite facing severe hunger and danger, Action Against Hunger staff continue to operate in the Gaza Strip, providing critical care to thousands of women and children. Nearly 400 children under five are currently receiving treatment for malnutrition in our clinics, the highest caseload since the conflict began.

Critical supplies for treating malnutrition are scarce. Therapeutic foods, supplements for infants, and micronutrients for pregnant women are running out. "A trickle of aid is not enough to sustain a population of two million people who have been on the brink of famine for almost two years," says Natalia Anguera. "We need all administrative barriers to the import of goods to be removed, all borders to be open and operational, and access to all areas of the Gaza Strip to be allowed."

A Call for Immediate and Unrestricted Action

Action Against Hunger reiterates its commitment to initiatives which facilitate impartial, rapid, and evidence-based humanitarian action that reaches the people who need it most.

Action Against Hunger calls on all parties to:

  • Secure an immediate and permanent ceasefire and release of hostages.

  • Ensure safe, regular and unrestricted access for humanitarian aid at all points across and within the Gaza Strip.

  • Fully reopen border crossings and corridors, including the restoration of commercial goods flows.

  • Give assurance of a humanitarian response system led by the UN and independent organizations.

***

Action Against Hunger leads the global movement to end hunger. We innovate solutions, advocate for change, and reach 21 million people every year with proven hunger prevention and treatment programs. As a nonprofit that works across over 55 countries, our 8,900 dedicated staff members partner with communities to address the root causes of hunger, including climate change, conflict, inequity, and emergencies. We strive to create a world free from hunger, for everyone, for good.

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Action Against Hunger on 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:

Spokesperson: Action Against Hunger

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/action-against-hunger

Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: Action Against Hunger

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1509 ET - Crude oil futures fall for a sixth consecutive session in choppy trade amid uncertainty about the impact of U.S. tariffs, Russia sanctions, and output increases by OPEC+. Reports of positive talks between the U.S. and Russia on the Ukraine war and a possible meeting between Presidents Trump and Putin "increase the chances of a ceasefire, and perhaps even an eventual peace deal, though talks could remain tricky and the outcomes uncertain," Eric Lee of Citi Research says in a note. "Our base case forecast remains for Brent to head toward the low-$60s/barrel by end-year amid softening markets" even if talks are slow, India partially reduces purchases of Russian oil and China picks up more discounted barrels, he says. "U.S. appetite to enforce could remain low due to worries about high oil prices." Brent falls 0.7% to $66.43 a barrel and WTI settles down 0.7% at $63.88. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0920 ET - Oil futures are modestly higher after a five-session losing streak driven by OPEC+ plans to raise output and concerns that U.S. tariffs, which take effect today for dozens of countries, will eventually crimp demand. Market views differ on whether India will or won't stop buying Russian crude, although the additional 25% U.S. tariff is still weeks away and Russia says Presidents Trump and Putin are expected to meet in coming days. Additional increases in OPEC production are the "overriding negative consideration," Ritterbusch says in a note. WTI is up 0.3% at $64.56 a barrel and Brent is 0.3% higher at $67.12. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0800 ET - Canadian Natural Resources' 2Q performance was as straight down the fairway as could be hoped for, and it continues to deliver, Raymond James' Michael Barth says. As such he view the quarter as neutral, with production in line with his and the consensus forecast and a 3% beat on adjusted funds from operations. Raymond James has an outperform call on the shares, which were last at C$43.12, down 2.8% year-to-date. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0517 ET - BP's fortunes are starting turn around after the energy giant delivered significantly stronger results along with positive news about a potential discovery in Brazil's Bumerangue field, Berenberg analysts write. They upgrade their rating on the stock to buy from hold and raise its target price to 500 pence from 385 pence. The oil major's outlook has been boosted by stronger free cash flow after it cut costs and lowered capital expenditure, they add. The incoming chairman will also review costs further while examining its portfolio. As net debt falls, BP should continue attractive buybacks in 2026, they add. Shares trade down 0.3% at 428.95 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Consolidated Edison 2Q Adjusted Basic EPS 67c
Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1433 ET - Gold futures gain ground, with jobless claims rising more than expected this week and the Bank of England cutting rates for a fifth time this year. The developments have investors thinking a rate cut in the U.S. will come soon. "The weak data spurred markets to increase bets that the Fed will cut rates at its September meeting and beyond," says ADM Investor Services in a note. Front-month gold futures rise 0.6% to $3,400.30 a troy ounce. That makes it four out of the past five sessions that gold finished higher.(kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1023 ET - The agriculture economy remains mixed, Corteva executives say. Low corn and soybean prices are hurting farmer profits, but growers are still shelling out for top-tier crop seed and pesticide products from Corteva to try and maximize their harvests. The company posts increased sales in its seed and pesticide sales in North America for its latest quarter. To boost farmer's fortunes, Corteva CEO Chuck Magro says increased China buying, new trade routes and more crops being used in biofuels are needed. "The market is certainly expecting a record harvest in the U.S.," Magro says on an analyst call. "We're getting close to harvest here in North America and opening up global markets to allow for trade of these critically needed crops would help American farmers continue to feed the world." (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

2210 ET - Malaysia's wood manufacturing industry is likely to remain under pressure in 2H due to weak global demand, a softer dollar against the ringgit and higher electricity costs, says Hong Leong IB analyst Jack Heng Shao Jun in a note. While tariff uncertainty has eased and resin prices are falling, margin concerns persist amid a challenging outlook, he says. Still, Evergreen Fibreboard stands out for its strong exposure to Middle East construction, particularly Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030, which is driving demand for furniture and wood-based fittings, he notes. Hong Leong maintains a neutral rating on the sector, citing a lack of re-rating catalysts. It pegs Evergreen Fibreboard as its preferred pick. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1922 ET - BlueScope is a better bet than Sims for Australian steel exposure heading into FY25 results later this month, and more broadly, Jefferies analysts Ramoun Lazar and Kai Erman say in a note. They reckon stronger U.S. spreads should more than offset any effect from softer Australian volumes for steelmaker BlueScope. Lazar and Erman say BlueScope is the only stock in their coverage where Jefferies sits above consensus for 2H FY25 and for FY26. They cite U.S. steel-market tailwinds. Metals recycler Sims remains tethered to scrap prices, which have been rangebound, and trading margins, they say. Their forecasts for Sims are slightly below consensus for 2H FY25, and materially lower for FY26 onwards. "We see little upside to existing volumes without M&A." Jefferies has a "buy" rating on BlueScope, and "underperform" rating on Sims. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Bacardi Launches Glass Recycling Program in Puerto Rico To Reduce Waste From Hospitality Sector
data.description:
HAMILTON, BERMUDA / ACCESS Newswire / August 7, 2025 / Family-owned spirits company Bacardi has partnered with recycling company, Reciclaje del Norte, and the Municipality of Cataño, to launch a glass recycling program for businesses in the area surrounding its BACARDĺ rum distillery in Puerto Rico.

The new initiative which launched in May, sees the weekly collection of spirits bottles from participating bars and restaurants in the Cataño Bay area. The collected glass is then crushed and reused to produce building blocks.

To date, more than 1.3 tons of glass has been collected through the scheme, making an important contribution to reducing glass waste in landfill, and at the same time helping raise awareness of the importance of recycling in Puerto Rico.

"As a family-owned business, we believe strongly in doing the right thing by giving back to our local community and helping protect the environment in Cataño," said Magaly Feliciano, Global Sustainability Director at Bacardi, who is based in Puerto Rico. "We're so proud of this project as it's a perfect example of how we can work together to make a meaningful difference."

The mayor of Cataño, Julio Alicea Vasallo, said "This fantastic glass recycling program is not just another initiative; it is an innovative effort that involves and includes our businesses in Cataño Bay in the construction of an economy where waste is not waste, but an opportunity for something more."

He continues, "Every bottle we recycle today contributes to building a stronger Cataño tomorrow. This program shows that, with perseverance and strategic partnerships, we can help protect our environment for generations to come."

This initiative is just one way in which Bacardi is giving back to the local community and reducing the impact of its production on the environment, with other actions including switching from oil to gas at its distillery to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and reusing water to cool stills which saves millions of gallons of water every year. Recognizing its efforts to protect the local bat population in Puerto Rico, the Bacardi site also holds Wildlife Habitat Council (WHC) Certification - the only organization to do so on the island.

For more on the Good Spirited actions Bacardi is taking to protect People & Planet, visit www.bacardilimited.com/CS.

###

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Bacardi-Martini, Inc. on 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:

Spokesperson: Bacardi-Martini, Inc.

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/bacardi-limited

Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: Bacardi-Martini, Inc.

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1037 ET - Hertz Global's fleet rotation has left it with more than 80% of its U.S. vehicles being less than a year old, CEO Gil West says on a call with analysts. The rental-car company has moved aggressively to swap out the fleet and realign it to better reflect consumer preferences, he says. As a result, depreciation per unit in 2Q hit $251, exceeding Hertz's goal of being below $300. Hertz took early actions to get pre-tariff pricing for its model year 2025 vehicles while selling older vehicles into a frothy market for used cars, the CEO says. The company now has a smaller but higher quality fleet, which should provide for better unit economics going forward, he says. "This approach proved effective, yielding strong cash proceeds and positioning us for more efficient growth," West says. Hertz Global rises 11%.(dean.seal@wsj.com)

0632 ET - Rheinmetall held back from lifting its medium-term guidance on Thursday but it is still set to raise it once the German government provides some clarity around its defense budget in September or October, mwb research Jens-Peter Rieck tells Dow Jones Newswires. The German arms maker reported a softer second quarter, but its underlying growth story remains clearly intact, he says. The stock's pullback is a good entry point for investors, especially considering the record backlog, the potential for a very strong second half, and the drivers for increased long-term demand, he adds. Free cash flow turned sharply negative, reflecting high capex and inventory build-up ahead of an expected second-half acceleration, Rieck says. Shares are down 5% at 1,694.50 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0545 ET - The trade deal between the European Union and the U.S hasn't yet brought any clarity or improvement for the German automotive industry, Hildegard Mueller, president of the German association of the automotive industry, says in a comment. The sectoral tariffs of 27.5% on passenger cars and automotive parts, which have been in force since April and May, respectively, remain in place and are a considerable burden on German car manufacturers and automotive suppliers as well as transatlantic trade, Mueller says. She calls on the U.S. to withdraw the sectoral automotive tariffs. It is important that the relief measures are implemented promptly, she adds. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0421 ET - Cathay Pacific's near-term profitability looks weak, Lorraine Tan, director at Morningstar, writes in a note. Inflationary pressures on staff, passenger and route expenses outweighed savings from lower fuel prices and aircraft charges in 1H, and these cost pressures are expected to persist in the near term, Tan says. Yield normalization was faster than expected, causing a sell-off after the results, and the share price still looks overvalued, Tan says. Morningstar lowers its 2025 estimates for passenger and cargo yields but raises its passenger capacity and load factor forecasts. Morningstar trims its fair value estimate by 9% to HK$9.70. Shares closed 3.0% lower at HK$10.52. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0415 ET - Rheinmetall's decision to back its full-year guidance is a conservative move, because its estimates don't yet cover the improvement in market potential that will come from rising global military spending, Morgan Stanley analysts Marie-Ange Riggio and Ross Law say. They expect Germany to convert framework contracts worth 22 billion euros into order intake by the end of the year. Rheinmetall, Germany's biggest arms maker, still has a chance to adjust its forecasts as necessary, they note. "We believe the unchanged guidance seems very much to be a case of conservatism," they write in a research note. The second quarter was soft for orders intake, sales and margin, they add. Shares are down 4.5% at 1,703.50 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0407 ET - Maersk's second-quarter results will probably be received strongly by the market, as performance has been significantly better than expected, especially at the earnings level, Jyske Markets senior analyst Haider Anjum writes. All divisions are outperforming, despite geopolitical uncertainty and low visibility in relation to macroeconomic developments, he says. However, it is the guidance upgrade that is perhaps grabbing more of the headlines, with earnings, free cash flow and container volume growth forecasts all lifted, he adds. Maersk's management will hold a conference call later, where Jyske will keep an eye on wording around the consequences of import duties to the U.S. and expectations for 2026 and beyond. Shares in the Danish shipping company rise 4.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Maersk's second-quarter results came in ahead of expectations, providing a tailwind into the second half on top of resilient demand and prompting management to increase 2025 guidance, JPMorgan analysts write. "Given the feedback from investors in recent days we think the market had expected an upgrade to expectations, and given this setup we do not expect a strong reaction to the shares [Thursday] morning, albeit likely still positive." The bank expects some near-term upgrades to 2025 consensus, but it still expects risks for 2026. The bank expects focus on the management call will be on the near-term outlook and expectations heading into 2026. Shares in the Danish shipping company rise 4.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0339 ET - Maersk's consensus earnings expectations could be revised modestly higher after second-quarter results beat expectations and full-year guidance was raised, Morgan Stanley's Cedar Ekblom and Peter Ajose-Adeogun write. The main ocean unit saw slightly higher loaded freight rates, better volumes and lower bunker consumption, while the logistics business posted better margins on cost control and commercial focus. In terminals, the company posted strong volumes and utilization rates at near capacity. The bank says it thinks the guidance upgrade might have been largely expected, considering firmer freight rates over the quarter linked to trade volatility and the need to reorganize capacity. A good quarter, which is already reflected in shares, the bank says. Shares rise 3.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0301 ET - Rheinmetall's shares might underperform Thursday after a weak second quarter, but the longer-term outlook of the German defense company remains strong, Berenberg analysts George McWhirter and Lavinia Norton say. The outlook is still the primary driver of the shares, they note. Rheinmetall is bracing for a significant acceleration in order intake in its defense business, they add. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0257 ET - Rheinmetall's second-quarter results were softer than anticipated, but the German defense group backed its full-year guidance and reiterated its expectations for a strong fourth quarter intake from Germany, Jefferies analysts Chloe Lemarie and Ben Brown say. Second-quarter sales of 2.43 billion euros were 4% below consensus, they note. The company's electronic solutions unit is showing weaker margins on year, they add. The main weak point of the release is the free cash outflow of 911 million euros, the analysts say. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0251 ET - Rheinmetall's second-quarter results were weak, but that was expected, JPMorgan analysts David Perry and Lucy Fitzgerald say. The German arms maker posted a free cash outflow of 911 million euros, which was 661 million euros lower than they expected. Although this figure doesn't look good, it is rather irrelevant to the investment case, they add. Germany didn't have a government for much of the second quarter, which delayed orders, the analysts note. Rheinmetall spent those months investing in growth and should reap the benefits in the second half of the year and beyond, the analysts say. The group should raise its medium-term guidance meaningfully later this year, they say. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1854 ET - This earnings season investors are likely to be relatively unforgiving toward New Zealand companies that qualify any improvement in earnings on an economic recovery or house price inflation, Forsyth Barr says. "After three years, companies have had the time to right size their businesses," analyst Aaron Ibbotson says. Forsyth Barr says Spark NZ, Freightways and Fletcher Building--as well as consumer discretionary companies--should give a good read on New Zealand's economy. "Our Pulse of NZ Survey, published in June 2025, suggested that outside of retail and construction, a painfully slow but steady recovery is taking place and that corporates expect this to continue," Forsyth Barr says. It expects cautiously optimistic outlook statements, with a focus on cost control, conservative balance sheet settings and operational improvement. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: AVITA Medical, Inc. Cuts 2025 View To Rev $76M-$81M >AVH.AX.AU
Harte Hanks Q2 revenue down 14.2%
data.description:
Overview

  • Harte Hanks Q2 2025 revenue falls 14.2% yr/yr to $38.6 mln

  • Company reports net loss of $0.3 mln, improved from $27.8 mln loss last year

  • EBITDA for Q2 2025 at $1.1 mln, down from $2.4 mln in prior year

Result Drivers

  • CUSTOMER CARE - Revenue decreased 4.4% due to timing fluctuations in specific programs, but segment remains profitable

  • FULFILLMENT & LOGISTICS - Revenue declined 11.6% due to elevated production costs and repositioning of smaller projects

  • MARKETING SERVICES - Revenue fell 28.7% due to customer attrition and cautious client spending

Key Details

Metric

Beat/Miss

Actual

Consensus Estimate

Q2 Revenue

$38.63 mln

Q2 EPS

-$0.05

data.title: US loan disbursements to recommission Michigan nuclear plant top $83 million
data.description:
The Trump administration on Thursday said it issued a fifth installment of a loan guarantee for Holtec International's Michigan nuclear plant, which the company hopes will be the first U.S. reactor to restart after shuttering.

The Department of Energy said it has now disbursed more than $83 million of the up to $1.52 billion loan guarantee for the 800-megawatt Palisades reactor.

The conditional loan guarantee was initiated by former President Joe Biden's Loan Programs Office to support nuclear energy, which generates virtually emissions-free power, to help satisfy rising electricity demand from data centers and artificial intelligence.

Since then Constellation Energy said it would reopen the former Three Mile Island nuclear power plant in Pennsylvania, widely known as the site of a partial meltdown in 1979 that chilled the nuclear industry. Constellation said in June that the plant could restart in 2027, about a year ahead of schedule.

Power company Entergy closed Palisades in 2022, after it operated for more than 50 years. It shut two weeks ahead of schedule over a glitch with a control rod, despite a $6 billion federal program to save reactors suffering from rising costs.

U.S. President Donald Trump signed executive orders in May to fast-track new nuclear power licenses, and overhaul the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, which issues them. The NRC last month approved Holtec's request to load fuel into the reactor.

But the NRC said then there are still several licensing actions under its review and requirements that need to be met before Palisades can restart under the original operating license, which expires in 2031.

Holtec is repairing steam generators at Palisades as the standard procedure for maintaining the units was not followed when the plant went into shutdown.

Holtec, which said in March it wanted to restart the plant in the fourth quarter of 2025, did not immediately respond to a request about whether that timing still stands.

data.title: Gen Digital raises annual revenue and profit outlook on cybersecurity demand
data.description:
Antivirus software provider Gen Digital raised its annual revenue and profit targets on Thursday, benefiting from strong demand for its cybersecurity tools.

A surge in high-profile digital breaches and a rise in online scams have prompted companies to strengthen their defenses against evolving threats, driving enterprise spending on cybersecurity solutions—even amid broader economic uncertainty.

Demand for advanced security tools has remained strong, particularly as artificial intelligence enables increasingly sophisticated hacking techniques.

Gen Digital, which owns well-known antivirus brands including Avast, Avira, AVG and Norton, now expects fiscal 2026 revenue between $4.8 billion and $4.9 billion, compared with its prior target of $4.7 billion to $4.8 billion.

The Tempe, Arizona-based company also topped estimates for the quarter ended July 4, posting first-quarter revenue of $1.26 billion—beating analysts’ average estimate of $1.19 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG.

Shares of the company were up about 2% in extended trading.

Adjusted earnings came in at 64 cents per share, above the 60-cent estimate. The company now expects annual adjusted earnings per share in the range of $2.49 to $2.56, compared with its earlier guidance of $2.46 to $2.54.

data.title: Wall Street ends with Nasdaq record, S&P 500 dip
data.description:
Key points:
  • Nasdaq notches record with 0.35% advance, S&P off ~0.08%, Dow down ~0.51%
  • Healthcare weakest S&P group; Utilities leads gainers
  • Dollar dips; gold gains ~0.9%; bitcoin up ~2%; crude dips
  • US 10-Year Treasury yield edges up to ~4.25%

WALL STREET ENDS WITH NASDAQ RECORD, S&P 500 DIP

While the Nasdaq composite and the Nasdaq 100 registered a record closing highs with small advances on Thursday, the S&P 500 ended slightly lower and the Dow was down 0.5% after a choppy session. Investors digested some weaker than expected earnings updates and monitored the latest tariff moves and worried about their impact on inflation.

For the Nasdaq composite, it was its 17th record close so far this year. Also in the records department, the Philadelphia Gold and Silver index (.XAU), an index of 30 precious metal mining companies, posted a record closing high, finally surpassing its previous record closing high established on April 8, 2011.

U.S. The U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency began collecting tariffs ranging from 10% to 50% at 12:01 a.m. EDT (0401 GMT) after weeks of suspense over final rates and frantic negotiations. The leaders of Brazil and India vowed not to be cowed by Trump's hardline bargaining position, even while their negotiators sought a reprieve from the highest tariff levels.

Chip stocks were some of the Nasdaq's biggest boosts on Thursday after Trump's announcement that tariffs of about 100% on imports of semiconductors would not apply to companies that are manufacturing in the U.S. or have committed to do so. This helped send the Philadelphia semiconductor index up 1.5%.

Software was a weak spot in technology, however, with shares of Fortinet tumbling 22% after it forecast third-quarter revenue below Wall Street estimates late on Wednesday, as businesses cut back on spending amid widespread macroeconomic uncertainty stemming from global trade tensions. Crowdstrike fell 5.9% in sympathy. Even software heavyweight Microsoft came under pressure with a 0.8% drop, making it the Nasdaq's biggest drag on the day.

Still, the S&P 500 tech sector did manage a record close.

Rosenblatt Securities equity sales trader Michael James said that Fortinet's weakness cast a shadow over the broader market as well as other software companies.

"There's an elevated sense of nervousness amongst people, not just for a fear of missing out for another leg higher, but just as much the fear of overstaying one's welcome and not wanting to be too late to trim positions should things potentially roll over further," James said.

While the broader indexes had looked strong at the open, Sahak Manuelian, managing director for global equities trading at Wedbush Securities said that "under the surface it wasn't as good." Manuelian added: "There's definitely been pockets of weakness in earnings today."

Along with weakness in software, he pointed to investor disappointment over reports from some consumer companies. Ralph Lauren ended down 6.5% after it cautioned that tariff-related costs would pressure its margins this year and said it was bracing to see how inflation was weighing on price-sensitive consumers.

Crocs shares plunged 29% after it warned that tariffs would cut into Q3 margins and were leading to elevated inventory. While the casual shoe company said it will raise some prices to offset tariff impacts, it could not raise all prices due to consumer caution.

Meanwhile, in its latest Survey of Consumer Expectations, the New York Federal Reserve found that Americans' longer-term inflation outlook deteriorated in July even as households boosted their views on the current and future state of their respective financial situations. Respondents said the expected level of inflation five years from now was 2.9% in July, rising from 2.6% in the prior month and the highest reading since March. And inflation expectations for a year from now rose to 3.1% from 3% in June, while three-year-ahead expected inflation held steady at 3%.

Here is your closing snapshot:

(Sinéad Carew, Terence Gabriel)

*****

3M Open's Golf 4 All Day Inspires Twin Cities Youth With Golf, Science and Skilled Trades
data.description:
NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / August 7, 2025 / 3M

Originally published on 3M News Center

The 3M Open, in partnership with 3M Community Impact and 3Mgives, successfully hosted its fourth-annual Golf 4 All Day on Tuesday, July 22, at TPC Twin Cities.

The event welcomed over 500 students from across the Minneapolis and St. Paul metro area, offering a unique introduction to golf while also highlighting the exciting world of science and skilled trades.

Collaborating with Tickets for Kids and local organizations such as Solomon Hughes Sr. Golf Academy, First Tee Minnesota, and the Sanneh Foundation, Golf 4 All Day created an atmosphere that celebrated the joy of golf for all ages.

The afternoon was packed with experiences designed to educate and entertain. Kids gained hands-on experience with golf through "club-in-hand" activations expertly led by First Tee - Minnesota. Additionally, the 3M Swing Lab presented by TaylorMade, the 3M Open Primrose Playground, and interactive demonstrations with the 3M Visiting Wizards, a STEM education program led by volunteer 3Mers, brought scientific principles to life in captivating and accessible ways.

The event also saw an impressive lineup of celebrities and personalities join the students, including Minnesota Timberwolves star Mike Conley, former NBA player Cole Aldrich, Minnesota Vikings legends Randall McDaniel and John Randle, former Vikings wide receiver Michael Floyd and former Minnesota Timberwolves player Wally Szczerbiak. Their presence added an extra layer of excitement and inspiration for the young attendees.

A significant focus of this year's Golf 4 All Day was its expanded emphasis on STEM and career pathways in the skilled trades, notably with the introduction of the 3M Safety Roadshow. This new initiative operates as a mobile training center, directly addressing the growing manufacturing skills gap in the U.S. and the anticipated retirement of a significant portion of the current skilled trade workforce.

The Roadshow showcases 3M's industrial safety expertise and personal protective equipment, or PPE, providing students with valuable hands-on advanced manufacturing activities and safety demonstrations.

"So far, we've inspired over 1,300 students on six stops across Alabama, Arkansas and Missouri," said Torie Clarke, Chief Public Affairs Officer at 3M. "Today, we hope that the Safety Roadshow and other activities will inspire you, our future workforce, to consider a career in STEM or the skilled trades."

The 3M Open and its partners extend their sincere gratitude to all the students, 3Mers, and 3M partners who made this year's Golf 4 All Day an overwhelming success. The event reinforced the positive impact of community collaboration and highlighted the endless possibilities within golf, science and skilled trades.

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from 3M on 3blmedia.com.

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/3m

Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: 3M

data.title: Field of Screams Marks 33 Years of Fear, Family, and Fall Tradition in Lancaster County
data.description:
(TheNewswire)

Click Image To View Full Size

The iconic haunted attractioncontinues to grow while staying true to its community roots andhands-on approach to horror

LANCASTER, PA — August 2025 — TheNewswire -What started on a family farm in Lancaster County three decades agohas become one of the most recognized haunted attractions in thecountry. This fall, Field of Screams celebratesits 33rd season — delivering the same handcrafted scares that madeit a fall tradition, while continuing to expand, innovate, and giveback.

Launched in 1993 by brothers Gene and Jim Schopf, Field of Screamsbegan with a single haunted hayride and a big idea. Today, it featuresfour full-scale attractions — the original Haunted Hayride, thecreepy Den of Darkness, the twisted Frightmare Asylum, and the outdoorhorror trail, Nocturnal Wasteland. The experience also includesExtreme Blackout, a one-night-only event known for pushing boundariesand attracting horror fans from across the country.

“It’s wild to think how far we’ve come,” said Gene Schopf,co-founder of Field of Screams. “We never set out to be the biggestor the best — we just wanted to build something memorable. The factthat people come back year after year, some from hours away, isincredibly humbling.”

Built
by Hand, Night After Night


Unlike many seasonal haunts, Field of Screams builds everything
on-site — from animatronics and set pieces to sound design and
costumes. The result is a completely immersive, evolving experience
that changes every year.


Each off-season, the creative team develops new scenes, characters,
and technical effects to keep things fresh. And behind the scares is a
dedicated crew — many of whom have been with the attraction for over
a decade — who treat the haunt as more than a job. For them, it’s
a shared passion that comes to life every fall.


“We have team members who started here as teenagers and now bring
their own kids to help out,” said Jim Schopf. “It’s a community,
not just a cast.”


National Recognition, Local Spirit


Field of Screams has been featured by USA
Today, The TravelChannel, BuzzFeed, andother national outlets — often landing on top-ten lists for hauntedattractions. But even with the media spotlight, the team behind thescenes keeps things grounded.

Throughout the years, Field of Screams has partnered with localnonprofits, hosted charity nights, and supported dozens of communitycauses — from youth sports to local fire departments. The attractionalso hosts the Zombie Fun Run, where proceeds help benefit the PABreast Cancer Coalition.

“At the end of the day, we’re part of Lancaster County,” saidGene. “And we take a lot of pride in that.”

Looking Ahead to the 2025 Season


Now in its 33rd year, Field of Screams is preparing for anotherrecord-breaking season. New attractions and scenes are already inplace, and the full calendar of fall events is available on theofficial website. From first-timers to returning fans, there’ssomething for every level of thrill-seeker.

Tickets and details for the 2025 season are available at www.fieldofscreams.com.

About Field of Screams

 Field of Screams is anationally recognized haunted attraction located in Mountville,Pennsylvania. Founded in 1993, it features four immersive experiencesand is known for its original design, local ties, and commitment togiving guests a one-of-a-kind Halloween season. Field of Screamscontinues to receive national attention for its creative approach tohorror and community-driven mission.

Media Contact:

Company: Field of Screams

Website: https://fieldofscreams.com

 Email: pr@fieldofscreams.com
